SUMMARY:
The term epigenetics was first coined in the 1940s by C.H Waddington, who famously proposed the concept of the “epigenetic landscape” (Figure 1)

--- SOURCE 4: A Brief History of Epigenetics - PMC - PubMed Central ---
URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3941222/

SUMMARY:
“Epigenetics” originally denoted processes that allow a single fertilized egg to develop into numerous highly differentiated tissues.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 5: Epigenetics - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics

SUMMARY:
The NIH "Roadmap Epigenomics Project", which ran from 2008 to 2017, uses the following definition: "For purposes of this program, epigenetics refers to both heritable changes in gene activity and expression (in the progeny of cells or of individuals) and also stable, long-term alterations in the transcriptional potential of a cell that are not necessarily heritable." In 2008, a consensus definition of the epigenetic trait, a "stably heritable phenotype resulting from changes in a chromosome without alterations in the DNA sequence," was made at a Cold Spring Harbor meeting. Taken to its extreme, the "epigenetic code" could represent the total state of the cell, with the position of each molecule accounted for in an _epigenomic map_, a diagrammatic representation of the gene expression, DNA methylation and histone modification status of a particular genomic region.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Study of DNA modifications that do not change its sequence

For other uses, see [Epigenetic (disambiguation)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetic_(disambiguation) "Epigenetic (disambiguation)").

[![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Epigenetic_mechanisms.png/250px-Epigenetic_mechanisms.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Epigenetic_mechanisms.png)

Epigenetic mechanisms

In biology, **epigenetics** is the study of changes in [gene expression](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gene_expression "Gene expression") that happen without changes to the DNA sequence.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-Epigenetics_2009_review-1) The Greek prefix _epi-_ (ἐπι- "over, outside of, around") in _epigenetics_ implies features that are "on top of" or "in addition to" the traditional (DNA sequence based) genetic mechanism of inheritance.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-science-2) Epigenetics usually involves a change that is not erased by cell division, and affects the regulation of gene expression.[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-3) Such effects on cellular and physiological traits may result from environmental factors, or be part of normal development.

The term also refers to the mechanism of changes: functionally relevant alterations to the [genome](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Genome "Genome") that do not involve mutation of the [nucleotide sequence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nucleotide_sequence "Nucleotide sequence"). Examples of mechanisms that produce such changes are [DNA methylation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DNA_methylation "DNA methylation") and [histone modification](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Histone_modification "Histone modification"), each of which alters how genes are expressed without altering the underlying [DNA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DNA "DNA") sequence.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-4) Further, non-coding RNA sequences have been shown to play a key role in the regulation of gene expression.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-5) Gene expression can be controlled through the action of [repressor proteins](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Repressor_protein "Repressor protein") that attach to [silencer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Silencer_(DNA) "Silencer (DNA)") regions of the DNA. These epigenetic changes may last through [cell divisions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_division "Cell division") for the duration of the cell's life, and may also last for multiple generations, even though they do not involve changes in the underlying DNA sequence of the organism;[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-pmid17522671-6) instead, non-genetic factors cause the organism's genes to behave (or "express themselves") differently.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-7)

One example of an epigenetic change in [eukaryotic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eukaryotic "Eukaryotic") biology is the process of [cellular differentiation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cellular_differentiation "Cellular differentiation"). During [morphogenesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Morphogenesis "Morphogenesis"), [totipotent](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Totipotent "Totipotent")[stem cells](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stem_cells "Stem cells") become the various [pluripotent](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pluripotent "Pluripotent")[cell lines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_line "Cell line") of the [embryo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Embryo "Embryo"), which in turn become fully differentiated cells. In other words, as a single fertilized [egg cell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Egg_cell "Egg cell") – the [zygote](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zygote "Zygote") – continues to [divide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mitosis "Mitosis"), the resulting daughter cells change into all the different cell types in an organism, including [neurons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neurons "Neurons"), [muscle cells](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Muscle_cells "Muscle cells"), [epithelium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epithelium "Epithelium"), [endothelium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Endothelium "Endothelium") of [blood vessels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blood_vessels "Blood vessels"), etc., by activating some genes while inhibiting the expression of others.[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-pmid17522676-8)

Definitions
-----------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Epigenetics&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definitions")]

The term _epigenesis_ has a generic meaning of "extra growth" that has been used in English since the 17th century.[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-9) In scientific publications, the term _epigenetics_ started to appear in the 1930s (see Fig. on the right). However, its contemporary meaning emerged only in the 1990s.[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-Moore_2015-10)

[![Image 5](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/EpigenByYear_1.png/250px-EpigenByYear_1.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:EpigenByYear_1.png)

Number of patent families and non-patent documents with the term "epigenetic*" by publication year

A definition of the concept of _epigenetic trait_ as a "stably heritable phenotype resulting from changes in a chromosome without alterations in the DNA sequence" was formulated at a [Cold Spring Harbor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cold_Spring_Harbor_Laboratory "Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory") meeting in 2008,[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-pmid19339683-11) although alternate definitions that include non-heritable traits are still being used widely.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-NIH-12)

### Waddington's canalisation, 1940s

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Epigenetics&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Waddington's canalisation, 1940s")]

The hypothesis of epigenetic changes affecting the expression of [chromosomes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chromosome "Chromosome") was put forth by the Russian biologist [Nikolai Koltsov](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nikolai_Koltsov "Nikolai Koltsov").[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-13) From the generic meaning, and the associated adjective _epigenetic_, British embryologist [C. H. Waddington](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/C._H._Waddington "C. H. Waddington") coined the term _epigenetics_ in 1942 as pertaining to _[epigenesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenesis\_(biology) "Epigenesis (biology)")_, in parallel to [Valentin Haecker](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Valentin_Haecker "Valentin Haecker")'s 'phenogenetics' (_Phänogenetik_).[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-waddington-14)_Epigenesis_ in the context of the biology of that period referred to the [differentiation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cellular_differentiation "Cellular differentiation") of cells from their initial [totipotent](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Totipotent "Totipotent") state during [embryonic development](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Embryonic_development "Embryonic development").[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-15)

When Waddington coined the term, the physical nature of [genes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gene "Gene") and their role in heredity was not known. He used it instead as a conceptual model of how genetic components might interact with their surroundings to produce a [phenotype](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenotype "Phenotype"); he used the phrase "[epigenetic landscape](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetic_landscape "Epigenetic landscape")" as a metaphor for [biological development](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Morphogenesis "Morphogenesis"). Waddington held that cell fates were established during development in a process he called [canalisation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Canalisation_(genetics) "Canalisation (genetics)") much as a marble rolls down to the point of [lowest local elevation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Local_optimum "Local optimum").[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-Waddington2014-16) Waddington suggested visualising increasing irreversibility of cell type differentiation as ridges rising between the valleys where the marbles (analogous to cells) are travelling.[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-17)

In recent times, Waddington's notion of the epigenetic landscape has been rigorously formalized in the context of the [systems dynamics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/System_dynamics "System dynamics") state approach to the study of cell-fate.[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-18)[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-sciencedirect.com-19) Cell-fate determination is predicted to exhibit certain dynamics, such as attractor-convergence (the attractor can be an equilibrium point, limit cycle or [strange attractor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strange_attractor "Strange attractor")) or oscillatory.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-sciencedirect.com-19)

### Contemporary

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Epigenetics&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Contemporary")]

In 1990, [Robin Holliday](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robin_Holliday "Robin Holliday") defined epigenetics as "the study of the mechanisms of temporal and spatial control of gene activity during the development of complex organisms."[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-pmid2265224-20)

More recent usage of the word in biology follows stricter definitions. As defined by [Arthur Riggs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arthur_Riggs_(geneticist) "Arthur Riggs (geneticist)") and colleagues, it is "the study of [mitotically](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mitosis "Mitosis") and/or [meiotically](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meiosis "Meiosis") heritable changes in gene function that cannot be explained by changes in DNA sequence."[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-isbn0-87969-490-4-21)

The term has also been used, however, to describe processes which have not been demonstrated to be heritable, such as some forms of histone modification. Consequently, there are attempts to redefine "epigenetics" in broader terms that would avoid the constraints of requiring [heritability](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heritability "Heritability"). For example, [Adrian Bird](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adrian_Peter_Bird "Adrian Peter Bird") defined epigenetics as "the structural adaptation of chromosomal regions so as to register, signal or perpetuate altered activity states."[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-pmid17522671-6) This definition would be inclusive of transient modifications associated with [DNA repair](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DNA_repair "DNA repair") or [cell-cycle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell-cycle "Cell-cycle") phases as well as stable changes maintained across multiple cell generations, but exclude others such as templating of membrane architecture and [prions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prions "Prions") unless they impinge on chromosome function. Such redefinitions however are not universally accepted and are still subject to debate.[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-nature2008-22) The [NIH](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Institutes_of_Health "National Institutes of Health") "Roadmap Epigenomics Project", which ran from 2008 to 2017, uses the following definition: "For purposes of this program, epigenetics refers to both heritable changes in gene activity and [expression](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gene_expression "Gene expression") (in the progeny of cells or of individuals) and also stable, long-term alterations in the transcriptional potential of a cell that are not necessarily heritable."[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epigenetics#cite_note-23) In 2008, a consensus definition of the epigenetic trait, a "stably heritable phenotype resulting from changes in a chromosome without alterations in the DNA sequence," was made at a [Cold Spring Harbor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cold_Spring_Harbor_Laboratory "Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory") meeting.[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org

enriched_points.expanded_content: The term 'epigenetics' was coined by C.H. Waddington in the 1940s, highlighting gene expression regulation beyond DNA sequence alterations.
Significant discoveries include DNA methylation's role in gene regulation, identified in the 1970s, and the elucidation of histone modifications in the 1990s.

Epigenetics evolved from developmental biology to a prominent discipline encompassing gene regulation, environmental influence, and transgenerational inheritance, culminating in modern epigenome mapping.
